content:
A tiny macOS menu-bar tool that arms on a hotkey and locks your Mac on the first mouse move, click, or keypress. I built it because I leave AI agents running and walk away from an unlocked screen.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The idea in one image: the machine watches itself while you are gone“I got up to use the washroom.”
That is the whole origin story. I was three or four agents deep, the usual afternoon: one refactoring a service, one grinding through tests, one doing something I had half-forgotten about in another tab. All of them typing away on my machine. And I needed to leave my desk for two minutes.
I stood up, took one step, and stopped.
Behind me was an unlocked Mac, mid-thought, with my terminals, my Slack, my half-written messages, my everything, sitting there for anyone who walked past. I am a bit of a privacy freak. I rotate my home WiFi password every month for no reason a normal person would accept. So the idea of walking away from a wide-open screen made my skin crawl.
The obvious fix is to lock it. Control-Command-Q, done. Apple has had that shortcut since High Sierra and it works fine (Apple’s own docs).
But I kept not doing it.
The small friction that mattered
Here is the thing nobody tells you about locking your screen every time you stand up: it is annoying enough that you stop.
When I lock, I have to unlock. Touch ID, or the password if the sensor is being moody. Fine, once. But twelve times a day, every time I refill my water or answer a question at the next desk, and the tax adds up. So like everyone, I quietly started skipping it “just for a second.” The second becomes a walk to the coffee machine. The walk becomes a meeting someone pulled me into on the way back.
My screen was protected exactly as often as I remembered to protect it, which is to say, badly.
I wanted the opposite deal. I wanted to leave the screen unlocked while I was still basically there, watching the agents from across the room, and have it slam shut the instant an actual human reached for it. Not lock because I walked away. Lock because someone else arrived.
Why nothing already did this
I went looking, because building something is the last resort and I know it.
Everything macOS ships is an idle timer. “Require password after the screen saver begins… after 5 minutes.” Hot corners. Screensaver gating. They all fire on inactivity: the machine locks when nothing has happened for a while (the mechanics are all here). That is backwards for my problem. An idle timer punishes me for reading a long log without touching the trackpad, and it does nothing about the person who sits down and starts clicking within the five-minute window. The one moment I actually care about, first contact, is the one moment an idle timer sleeps through.
The third-party tools are all solving a cousin of my problem, never mine.

• Warden blocks input so your screen stays visible while a render or an agent runs. It ignores input on purpose. I wanted the reverse: notice input, then hide everything.

• iAlertU turns your MacBook into a car alarm with the motion sensor. It shrieks, it does not lock.

• Unplug Alarm goes off when someone yanks your charger or a USB stick.

• Find My Mac is for after the laptop is already gone.
Input blockers, motion alarms, theft recovery. Adjacent, adjacent, adjacent. Nobody had “arm now, and lock on the first human touch.” So I built it in an evening and named it MacLock, which is a boring name for a boring tool, which is the point.
What it actually does
It lives in the menu bar. No Dock icon, no window, no settings screen you will ever open twice. There is one lock glyph up top and that is the entire interface.
You press a hotkey. Default is Command-Option-L. It does not lock. It armssss...
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Three states. The hotkey moves you from idle to armed; the first touch does the restThe menu-bar glyph changes so I can read the state from across the room. Closed padlock means idle. Open padlock means it is arming, a two-second grace window so that the act of pressing the shortcut and pulling my hand back does not immediately trip it. Then an eye, which means it is armed and watching.
While that eye is up, the moment the mouse moves past a small tolerance, or a click or scroll or key arrives, the screen locks and the tool disarms itself. I come back, unlock once, and it is idle again, waiting for me to arm it before the next walk.
That is the whole product. Arm before you leave. It watches. First touch, it is gone.
One thing that tripped up a friend when I explained this: a locked Mac is not a sleeping Mac. Locking gates the screen and the keyboard, it does not touch the CPU. The terminals keep scrolling, the builds keep building, the agents keep grinding away behind the login prompt. The only thing that actually pauses that work is the machine going to sleep, which is a separate setting entirely (the distinction bites people all the time). So I keep mine plugged in with system sleep off. The lock is a curtain over the screen, never a pause on the work. That is the entire reason this approach is worth anything: if locking stopped the agents, I would just lock it and lose ten minutes of progress every coffee run.
The one clever bit, and where it stops
I did not want MacLock to demand permissions. The second an app asks for Accessibility or Input Monitoring, most people, correctly, get suspicious of a random security tool. A screen-locker that wants to read your keystrokes is exactly the thing a paranoid person should refuse.
So the detection leans on a small trick: you can poll the mouse position from anywhere on macOS without any special permission at all. NSEvent.mouseLocation, checked about 25 times a second, no prompt, no entitlement (it is right there in the docs). When you arm it, it records where the cursor is. If the cursor ever moves more than three percent of the screen's diagonal from that spot, that is a human, and it locks.
Why three percent and not zero? Because a cursor is never truly still. Optical sensors drift, the desk gets bumped, the number wobbles by a pixel or two on its own. A zero-tolerance version would lock the instant you armed it. The three percent is a jitter cushion, small enough to catch a real hand on the trackpad, wide enough to ignore the noise.
Here is the honest seam, because the tool has one. Reading mouse motion is free. Globally catching keystrokes is not: macOS makes you grant Accessibility for that, by design (Apple’s event-monitoring guide spells out the split). MacLock does register a bonus monitor for clicks and scrolls and keys, but the load-bearing detector, the one that works with zero permissions, is the mouse poll. And in practice that is enough, because a person who sits down at your machine reaches for the trackpad before they do anything else. The mouse moves first. I am leaning on a human habit to cover a permission I refused to ask for. If someone never touched the trackpad and somehow typed blind, an unpermissioned MacLock would miss them. I decided I could live with that.
The actual locking uses a private Apple symbol, SACLockScreenImmediate, the same one the menu-bar "Lock Screen" item calls under the hood (reverse-engineered here). It is a real lock, not a screensaver: the login screen comes up and it wants a password before it lets anyone back in. "Private" also means undocumented, which means Apple owes me nothing and could rename it in any macOS update. So there is a fallback path, and I will find out it broke the way everyone finds out: one day it just will not lock, and I will go read a changelog. That is the rent on a door Apple did not officially leave open.
Does it work
A couple of times I have come back from the coffee machine to a screen that was already locked.
I do not know who. It does not take a photo, and I never built it to. Somebody reached for my trackpad, the eye caught them, the machine shut its own mouth, and by the time I walked back it was sitting there innocent behind the lock screen. Nothing dramatic. If anything the opposite of drama: word travels a floor quietly, and these days nobody really reaches for a machine that so plainly locks the second you touch it. Which is the actual win. I never wanted to catch anyone. I wanted them to not try.
Do one thing
I could bolt on more. A timer, a camera snapshot, a log of trip times, a menu of tolerances, iCloud sync of my preferences. I am not going to. The value of this thing is that it does one job and asks nothing of me. I arm it on the way up from my chair, the way you pat your pocket for your keys, and then I stop thinking about it.
The code is on GitHub if you want to read it or build your own: github.com/app-healer/mac-lock. It is small enough to read over a coffee, which is the highest compliment I can pay my own software.
Here is the actual download link if you really want to try it out without any fuss of building it.
I still get up for water more than I probably should. The difference is that now, on the way, I tap two keys, the eye comes up, and I do not look back at the screen even once.
